Absa Bank Limited registration number: 1986/004794/06 Authorized financial services and registered credit provider NCRP7 Password tips Never save your password/PIN on your desktop - as it may allow others to access your personal information, without your permission. Do not make your passwords too personal - rather create passwords that have letters and numbers that cannot be attributed to you. Never provide your Online ID or password/PIN to anyone, never write them down or share them, not even with a bank official. Never provide your Online ID or password/PIN to anyone. Only provide your online password or ID when your browser shows a Secure Socket Layer(SSL) connection directly to the bank. Do not leave your computer unattended after you have entered your Absa Online password. Always log or sign off at the end of a session. For your security Do not open e-mails from unknown sources. Beware of emails that ask for passwords, PINS, credit and debit card information. Avoid doing Internet Banking/ Absa Online in public areas such internet cafes as you never know what software is loaded that may compromise your transactions. Make sure no one has unauthorised access to your PC. Be especially aware that there are no security cameras trained on your PC and keyboard. Ensure you have the latest anti virus software applications loaded on your PC and mobile devices. Make sure the software on your PC is correctly licensed. Update your operating system and browser with latest patches. Install personal firewall on your PC if you use a dial-up modem. Only provide your credit card details to reputable companies. To ensure that you are using a secure shopping site, look for the lock and key and security certificates online. Subject: What's Cool And New In Home And Kitchen
Body: Forbes Plus: These Are The 35 Best Sales Online Right Now All Products and services are independently selected by Forbes Vetted contributors and editors. When you make a purchase through links in this email, we may earn a commission. Launch Party: The Latest Home And Kitchen Products To Shop In May It’s time to pack away your puffers and pull out your Birkenstocks. This month’s home and kitchen launches are giving us an excuse to stock up on summer-appropriate goodies that will help us make the most of this seasonal transformation. There’s everything you need for the warmer months ahead, starting with brightly colored towels from Brooklinen that you can proudly tote to the beach or pool, a high-quality outdoor grilling set from Made In that not only looks great, but can churn out delicious meals, and a new olive oil brand to join the burgeoning bougie options that are already gracing our pantry shelves. Read The Story ADVERTISEMENT Shop The Story Brooklinen Wavelength Bath Sheets Made using 100% Turkish cotton, these plush towels feature vibrant wavy patterns in three cheery colors, perfect for the beach or bathing at home. The brand is offering a matching robe as well for anyone looking to complete the set.$80 From Brooklinen Made In The Griddle System If you enjoy taking your grilling game with you on outdoor adventures, Made In’s Griddle System is for you. Subject: Re: selective greylisting based on user preference
Body: On Feb 10, 2008, at 10:43 AM, Dennis Trabjerg wrote: > I run the companyÂ´s mailserver and would like to be able to > implement greylisting on selected mailaccounts. > > Users should be able to choose greylisting as an option per. username. > > So basically i need postfix to do a quick lookup in a MySQL table to > see if the username has opted for greylisting and then, if yes, > greylist the mail via either postgrey or SQLgrey. > > How would you guys (if possible and reasonable) go about > implementing such a solution ? I use postgrey, and there's are whitelists in the config files in /etc/ postgrey. You can whitelist senders or receivers. -- Glenn English uos@slsware.com
text:
Re: selective greylisting based on user preference

On Feb 10, 2008, at 10:43 AM, Dennis Trabjerg wrote: > I run the companyÂ´s mailserver and would like to be able to > implement greylisting on selected mailaccounts. > > Users should be able to choose greylisting as an option per. username. > > So basically i need postfix to do a quick lookup in a MySQL table to > see if the username has opted for greylisting and then, if yes, > greylist the mail via either postgrey or SQLgrey. > > How would you guys (if possible and reasonable) go about > implementing such a solution ? I use postgrey, and there's are whitelists in the config files in /etc/ postgrey. You can whitelist senders or receivers. -- Glenn English uos@slsware.com

Subject: Career Lessons From Taylor Swift
Body: Forbes NewslettersPlus: How America’s Property Barons Plan To Survive The Commercial Real Estate CrisisADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesWelcome back from the long weekend! Looking forward to the four-day workweek? For many employees, it’s already a year-round reality. Whether you leveraged the long weekend to catch up on Succession, see Taylor Swift in concert or finally finish reading that book you’ve been putting off, three-day weekends can do wonders for your career growth and help set your focus for the workweek.This week, we hope your four-day workweek includes joining us for our second Forbes Future of Work Summit Thursday, which I will be covering live. You can register to attend virtually here.Hope your week is productive and balanced. Emmy Lucas Careers General Assignment ReporterFollow me on Twitter and Forbes.comWORK SMARTERPractical insights and advice from Forbes contributors to help you succeed in your job, accelerate your career and lead smarterPhone interviews can be tricky. But there are ways to make the best of them.If you’re feeling overlooked at work, here are six steps you can take to boost your visibility.If you’ve ever seen Taylor Swift in concert, you know there’s a lot to take in—including leadership lessons.Self-employed workers know scheduling your own hours can be challenging. Here are six tricks.Stop staring at the blank page, and put this cover letter advice to paper.Featured StoryUnion Station in Washington, D.C., has gone from bustling to battered as millions of commuters stay home—forever. COLIN WINTERBOTTOMStay Alive Until 2025: How America’s Property Barons Plan To Survive The Commercial Real Estate CrisisRead ArticleWhat’s happening to office towers, retail outlets and transportation centers as vacancies rise, commuters continue to work from home and interest rates remain high? Forbes’ Giacomo Tognini and Richard Chang speak with real estate moguls about their future plans and commercial casualties as the value of commercial real estate plummets from New York to San Francisco.TOUCH BASEAccording to Gallup, 7.2% of Americans identify as LGBTQIA+.GETTYNews from the world of work‘Queer-friendly’ workplaces: Employer diversity efforts can often feel performative. As Pride Month approaches, it’s important to remember LGBTQ+ people should be seen, heard and represented all year round—not just in June, writes contributor Maya Richard-Craven, who provides three ways you can make your workplace a more accepting place, whether as a manager or co-worker. Making a social network: Disenchanted with social media options like Twitter, the reclusive Bitcoin programmer @Fiatjaf tells Forbes how he designed his chat-and-payment service for people who dislike corporate control. Meet the mysterious Nostr creator who helped the network lure 18 million users and $5 million from Jack Dorsey.Is the ‘follow your passions’ advice more harmful than helpful?: Early career workers are often advised to follow their passions. However, a new study shows that this advice may narrow people’s career choices and contribute to the lack of women in STEM fields, senior contributor Kim Elsesser reports. The research found that when both students and adults were advised to follow their passions, they chose fields that were more consistent with what was expected of their gender. Layoff tracker: JPMorgan Chase laid off roughly 1,000 employees from failed lender First Republic, three weeks after JPMorgan acquired the collapsed regional bank. Meta continues its rolling layoffs, informing about 6,000 they had been let go as of last week. And more.Student loan repayments to resume: The Biden administration’s pause on federal student loan repayments will end in late August as part of the debt ceiling deal President Joe Biden and House Speaker Kevin McCarthy agreed to Sunday.ADVERTISEMENTCHECKLISTAdd these books and videos to your to-do listIn The Good Enough Job: Reclaiming Life from Work, author Simone Stolzoff explores why work has become so central to our identities—and how to reclaim our lives beyond our jobs.Grammarly CEO Rahul Roy Chowdhury joins "Forbes Talks" to discuss why he's taken the lead on the growing software company, the risk of training ChatGPT to disrupt what he does and more.Contributor Ruth Gotian highlights six “must-read” books for any stage of your career.NUMBER TO NOTE0.7%The percentage home prices were up year-over-year in March.After seven months of falling, home prices rose for a second straight month in March, S&P CoreLogic reported Tuesday, signaling the stretch of price declines may have come to an end. Forbes has advice for homebuyers.QUIZFollowing in Florida’s footsteps, legislators in which state passed a bill that would ban diversity, equity and inclusion efforts at public universities? If signed, the bill would prohibit DEI offices, programs and trainings at public colleges—and would require all hiring practices be “color-blind and sex-neutral.”A.AlabamaB.TexasC.OklahomaD.Wisconsin Check if you got it right here.ACROSS THE NEWSROOMForbes' Future of Work newsletter features headlines, insights and ideas about where the world of work is headed next. Sign-up instantly here and we'll see you in your inbox on Fridays.Forbes is looking for bold changemakers to highlight as part of ten new Under 30 Local lists in the vibrant innovation hubs of Atlanta, Austin, Boston, Chicago, Cleveland, Los Angeles, Miami, Puerto Rico, Seattle and Toronto. The deadline to nominate someone (or yourself!) is June 9.Thanks for reading! Follow us on Twitter for by-the-minute updates on the latest business and financial news throughout the day. Memorial Day Sale: Save 50%Unlock the tools and connections you need to succeed. Become a member today for just $39.99. Get unlimited access to premium journalism, exclusive events and more.Sale Ends Soon: Save 50% Unsubscribe from Careers.Manage Email Preferences My Forbes Account | Newsletters | Email Help | PrivacyForbes Media 499 Washington Blvd. Jersey City, NJ 07310

Subject: Call for Paper: ICATES & ICABEH 2026 - April 24-26, Izmir/Turkiye
Body:
Dear Colleague,

We are delighted to invite you to the 3rd International Conference on Advances in Technology, Education and Science (ICATES) and the 2nd International Conference on Advances in Basic Sciences, Engineering and Health (ICABEH) organized by ARSTE.

The conference will take place at Best Western İzmir Hotel on April 24-26, 2026 in Izmir, Türkiye. The conference will be held both in-person and virtually via Zoom.

After peer-review, accepted papers will be published in a proceedings book with ISBN. Proceedings will be submitted to databases such as Scopus, Web of Science, and ERIC for indexing. Journal and book publications are free for conference participants.

Registration deadlines:
- Regular registration ends February 15, 2026
- Final registration ends March 15, 2026

The venue is centrally located in İzmir with access to historical and cultural sites including Ephesus, the House of the Virgin Mary, Çeşme, and Alaçatı.

Supported journals include:
- International Journal of Academic Studies in Technology and Education (IJASTE)
- International Journal of Academic Studies in Science and Education (IJASSE)

For more information or to register, visit the official websites.

Contact via WhatsApp or Telegram: +905069167881

Copyright © 2026
